pattern: Because each update only changes a single position, only the (at most two) adjacent pairs that touch that position can change the running same-color-neighbor count, so maintain that count incrementally instead of rescanning the whole array after every update.
hints: Track a running total of how many adjacent pairs currently share a non-zero color; recompute it from scratch every query only if you don't mind the slower approach.

Before overwriting a position, subtract from the running total any adjacent pair that currently matches because of that position's old value; after overwriting, add back any adjacent pair that now matches because of the new value.

Only the pair (index-1, index) and the pair (index, index+1) can possibly change when you write to index, so the update is O(1) per query: check up to two neighbors before the write and the same two after.

edge_inputs.description: single element array, one query, no neighbors possible
sequence of queries building up matching neighbors then breaking them
re-coloring the same index repeatedly must correctly remove the old color's contribution each time
coloring the two ends of a longer array which are never adjacent to each other
alternating colors that never match at the boundary between two already-colored runs

stress.description: large n with many queries at random indices and a small color palette to encourage repeated matches
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = size
    m = size
    queries = [[rng.randint(0, n - 1), rng.randint(1, 5)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [n, queries]

stress.description: large n with a full color palette so matches are rarer, testing the O(1)-per-query path under load
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = size
    m = size
    queries = [[rng.randint(0, n - 1), rng.randint(1, 100000)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [n, queries]
